elements.sent-2411-001: We consider the problem of allocating heterogeneous and indivisible goods among strategic agents, with preferences over subsets of goods, when there is no medium of exchange.
elements.sent-2411-002: This model captures the well studied problem of fair allocation of indivisible goods.
elements.sent-2411-003: Serial-quota mechanisms are allocation mechanisms where there is a predefined order over agents, and each agent in her turn picks a predefined number of goods from the remaining goods.
elements.sent-2411-004: These mechanisms are clearly strategy-proof, non-bossy, and neutral.
elements.sent-2411-005: Are there other mechanisms with these properties? We show that for important classes of strict ordinal preferences (as lexicographic preferences, and as the class of all strict preferences), these are the only mechanisms with these properties.
elements.sent-2411-006: Importantly, unlike previous work, we can prove the claim even for mechanisms that are not Pareto-efficient.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we generalize these results to preferences that are cardinal, including any valuation class that contains additive valuations.
elements.sent-2411-008: We then derive strong negative implications of this result on truthful mechanisms for fair allocation on indivisible goods to agents with additive valuations.
elements.sent-2411-009: One of the central goals of Algorithm Mechanism Design is understanding the extent in which incentives impact the quality of the outcome of algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-010: A central problem that has attracted significant research effort is the problem of fairly allocating indivisible goods, mainly from the algorithmic point of view (when the input is assumed to be known); see, for example, \cite{moulin2004fair, LMMS04, KurokawaPW18,caragiannis2019envy, PR2020, ChaudhuryKMS21, Amanatidis2023, ChaudhuryGM24 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The challenge of incorporating incentives for truthful reporting\footnote{{Our work focuses on dominant strategies, so ``truthful'' (or ``strategy-proofness'') is in the sense of truthful reporting being a dominant strategy.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Unfortunately, that result does not imply a result beyond that special case, and no results are known beyond that special case, except when also requiring the mechanism to be Pareto-efficient (a strong requirement).
elements.sent-2411-013: This paper aims to make progress on this important and challenging problem of understanding the limits of fair allocation algorithms that incentivize truthful reporting without assuming Pareto-efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-014: Specifically, we consider the problem of allocating heterogeneous and indivisible goods among agents, with either ordinal or cardinal preferences over subsets of goods, with no medium of exchange (no transfers).
elements.sent-2411-015: This captures, for example, the prominent model of fair division of heterogeneous and indivisible goods.
elements.sent-2411-016: In many of these settings, some allocation algorithm is used to decide on the allocation of goods to agents, given the agents' preferences.
elements.sent-2411-017: When preferences are private information, agents may act strategically to optimize their allocation.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, consider allocating goods to agents with additive valuations using the Round Robin protocol (agents cyclically pick a good from the remaining goods).
elements.sent-2411-019: When all agent have the same preference it is always best to pick items in decreasing order of value.
elements.sent-2411-020: Yet picking in that order is not a dominant strategy: when preferences are different, there is no reason to take desirable goods early if no other agent wants them.
elements.sent-2411-021: As in many settings agents may act strategically, understanding which allocation algorithms are not susceptible to manipulations (are ``strategy-proof'') is fundamental.
elements.sent-2411-022: Such understanding will also give insights regarding the implications of incentives on the fairness of algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-023: The seminal work of Amanatidis et al. \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 has studied the fair partition\footnote{While an allocation mechanism might leave some goods unassigned, a partition mechanism must allocate all goods.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: First, they have presented a characterization of truthful partition mechanisms for two agents (in partition mechanisms, all goods must be allocated).
elements.sent-2411-025: Then, from the characterization, they derived strong impossibility results for fair partition of indivisible goods for two agents with additive valuations.
elements.sent-2411-026: We stress that, somewhat unintuitively, the impossibility for two agents when all goods must be allocated does not immediately imply an impossibility for more agents or allocation mechanisms\footnote{Throughout the paper we use the term ``allocation mechanisms'' for mechanisms that can leave goods unallocated, and use ``partition mechanisms'' for mechanisms that must allocate all goods.. .}
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, it does not imply any impossibility for the case of three agents or even for the case of two agents when not all goods must be allocated.
elements.sent-2411-028: When not all goods must be allocated, there may be some mechanism that ``throws away" some of the goods to create a fairer allocation than when all goods are assigned.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus, the requirement to partition all goods into the two agents prevents a simple reduction from more than two agents to the two agents' case.
elements.sent-2411-030: We thus ask: What are the limits of fairness when allocating goods to strategic agents with private preferences? To address this, it is natural to first ask: Which mechanisms for allocating goods to strategic agents are strategy-proof (truthful)? Indeed, similarly to Amanatidis et al. \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 we approach the first question by initially aiming to address the second.}
elements.sent-2411-031: So, first, we seek a characterization of truthful mechanisms and then address the first question using the fact that every mechanism in the limited set of truthful mechanisms has poor fairness guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-032: Addressing the characterization question is challenging, as even when one restricts attention to consider only the case of partition mechanisms for two agents, the characterized truthful mechanisms (as presented by \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 ) are very involved.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Currently, extending this characterization without any additional assumptions seems out of reach.
elements.sent-2411-034: We thus restrict our attention to mechanisms that do not care about the naming of the goods.
elements.sent-2411-035: These are called neutral allocation mechanisms in the literature (or in the context of shares, they are called name-independent in \cite{BF22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: Central fairness notions do not depend on good names, for example, the maximin share (MMS)\cite{Budish11 , and the notion of envy-free-up-to-one-good (EF1)~\cite{Budish11,LMMS04 .\footnote{See \cref{sec:implications-fair for the definitions of MMS and EF1.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Similarly, commonly used allocation mechanisms, such as the Round Robin algorithm\footnote{In the Round Robin algorithm agents cycle picking goods in turns.}
elements.sent-2411-038: There are other (non-truthful) neutral algorithms for fair allocation, including ones with constant MMS approximation.
elements.sent-2411-039: When restricting to neutral mechanisms, the characterization of \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 boils down to mechanisms in which, for some $k$, the first agent picks $k$ goods, and the other gets the rest.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Observe that these mechanisms are actually ``serial-quota" partition mechanisms for two agents.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this paper, we ask for a characterization result beyond two agents and when allowing the mechanism to leave goods unallocated, and as serial-quota mechanisms play a central role in our result, we next discuss these mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-042: A serial-quota mechanism is a mechanism where there is a predefined order over agents and a predefined quota (number of goods) for each agent, where both the order and quotas are independent of preferences.
elements.sent-2411-043: Each agent, in turn, according to the predefined order, picks a set of goods no larger than her quota from the remaining goods.
elements.sent-2411-044: When agents have strict preferences over sets of goods, these simple mechanisms have several good properties.
elements.sent-2411-045: First, each agent has a dominant strategy (so the mechanism is strategy-proof): she should pick a set of size equal to her quota that she likes best (her demand) among the remaining goods.
elements.sent-2411-046: Second, the mechanism is non-bossy: an agent cannot impact the set received by any other agent, without affecting her own allocation.\footnote{We note that while \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 did not explicitly assume that the mechanism is non-bossy, their assumptions that there are only two agents and that all items must be partitioned, imply non-bossiness.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this paper we explicitly make the assumption that the mechanism is non-bossy.
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, the mechanism is neutral: if all agents rename the goods, the allocation remains the same (the class is ``permutations-closed").\footnote{See \cref{sec:mechanisms for formal definitions of these properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, every such mechanism might be very unfair to some of the agents.
elements.sent-2411-050: Importantly, for a given serial-quota mechanism, the ordering of the agents and the quotas must be fixed ``a-priori'', that is, they are independent of the reports of the agents.
elements.sent-2411-051: Unfortunately, for whatever quotas are chosen, there will be preferences for which the mechanism’s allocation is very unfair.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example, consider partitioning 1000 goods among five agents with additive valuations over goods.
elements.sent-2411-053: If the quotas of the first four agents are larger than 1, in a setting where the fifth agent only values 5 of goods (say identically), the mechanism might give her a bundle of no value to her (if the others pick all 5 goods she likes).
elements.sent-2411-054: This outcome seems unfair as she should get at least one of these five goods (her MMS is not zero).
elements.sent-2411-055: So the only serial-quota partition mechanism that gives her any value is the one in which each of the first four agents gets one good, and the fifth agent gets all the rest.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, this mechanism is very unfair when all agents value all goods identically.
elements.sent-2411-057: We thus ask: Are there any other mechanisms that are strategy-proof, non-bossy, and neutral? Such mechanisms, in particular, might be able to get better fairness properties.
elements.sent-2411-058: A related but easier problem was addressed in \cite{Papai2000 , which showed that for agents with monotone and strict preferences, serial-quota mechanisms are the only mechanisms if, in addition to strategy-proof, non-bossy, and neutral, the mechanism must also be Pareto-efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-059: A mechanism is Pareto-efficient if the allocation it picks cannot be improved (one agent is strictly better off, while no other is harmed).
elements.sent-2411-060: Pareto-efficiency is a very strong requirement, which is violated by many mechanisms, including serial-quota mechanisms for most quotas (they are PO only if quotas of all agents except the first are either 0 or 1 \cite{Papai2000 ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: Consider for example allocating three goods to two additive agents, when the first agent picks a single good, and the second agent gets the remaining two goods.
elements.sent-2411-062: The allocation of this serial-quota mechanism is not necessarily Pareto-efficient, as evident from the following simple example.
elements.sent-2411-063: Assume that the first agent values the goods at (11,10,10), while the second agent values the goods at (10,1,1).
elements.sent-2411-064: The mechanism results with the first agent getting utility of 11, and the second agent getting utility of 2, while if they switch their sets both will be better off: the first agent will get 20, while the second agent will get 10.
elements.sent-2411-065: More generally, \cite{Papai2000 observed that a serial-quota mechanism is Pareto-efficient if and only if all goods are always allocated, and the quotas of all agents except the first are either 0 or 1.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Thus, imposing Pareto-efficiency on a serial-quota mechanism implies that not only does the first picker pick goods before others, but she also picks more goods than later agents.
elements.sent-2411-067: This seems very unfair, even in non-pathological examples, as when all goods are identical.
elements.sent-2411-068: We thus move to study truthful mechanisms that are not restricted to be Pareto-efficient, starting with characterizing such mechanisms that are neutral and non-bossy.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Our Main Result: Ordinal Mechanism Characterization
elements.sent-2411-069: We first consider agents with ordinal preferences and study the problem of allocating a set $\MM$ of $m$ goods among a set $\NN$ of $n$ agents, each with an ordinal preference over subsets of goods.
elements.sent-2411-070: A mechanism is a function that maps preferences of the $n$ agents to an allocation of the set of goods $\MM$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Preferences over $\MM$ come from a class of ordinal preferences $\PP= \PP(\MM)$, and as items are goods, preferences are weakly monotone.
elements.sent-2411-072: Moreover, we assume that preferences are strict.
elements.sent-2411-073: That is, for any two distinct sets, one must be strictly preferred over the other.
elements.sent-2411-074: Our main result is for strict preferences (no ties), showing that under mild assumptions about the class of preferences (discussed below), a strategy-proof, non-bossy, and neutral mechanism must be a serial-quota mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-075: Our result holds even if the mechanism is allowed not to allocate all goods.
elements.sent-2411-076: Moreover, our characterization result has significant implications for the setting that agents have cardinal valuation functions over all subsets of goods, and truthfulness is with respect to reporting of such functions.
elements.sent-2411-077: Although a cardinal valuation function encapsulates more information than an ordinal preference, we show that for important classes of strict valuations over goods, the characterization of truthful, non-bossy, and neutral mechanisms is the same as the one for ordinal preferences.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our main result applies, in particular, to the well-known class of additive valuation functions (without ties), as well as classes that contain them (such as the well-studied classes of sub-additive, sub-modular, and XOS valuation functions, but also the class of super-additive valuation functions).
elements.sent-2411-079: A central implication of the characterization is that for additive valuations, truthful, non-bossy, and neutral mechanisms have poor fairness properties (we discuss this in more details later, in \cref{sec:intro-imp ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: The two assumptions we make about the class of strict preferences $\PP$ are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-081: First, we assume that all lexicographic preferences are in the class.
elements.sent-2411-082: This holds for many natural classes, such as the class of preferences induced by additive valuations (and any superset of it, as the class of sub-modular or sub-additive valuations) and the larger class of all monotone preferences.
elements.sent-2411-083: Second, we assume that the class is permutations-closed; that is, for every preference in the class, it holds that all its permutations are also in the class (See \cref{definition:permuted_preference for the formal definition and a more detailed discussion).}
elements.sent-2411-084: This assumption is satisfied by most natural classes of preferences as it only requires that the names of goods are irrelevant (e.g., by the class of additive valuations and by the class of sub-additive valuations).
elements.sent-2411-085: For a set of goods $\MM$, we denote the family of classes of strict preferences that are permutations-closed and contain the entirety of the lexicographic preferences by $\FFn(\MM)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Our main result is a characterization of truthful, non-bossy, and neutral allocation mechanisms as serial-quota mechanisms, for every class in this wide family of classes of strict preferences (classes in $\FFn(\MM)$).
elements.sent-2411-087: Mechanism $f$ is truthful, non-bossy, and neutral if and only if $f$ is a serial-quota mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-088: We observe that each of the three properties is needed for the characterization to hold.
elements.sent-2411-089: A mechanism that is non-bossy and neutral but not truthful, could give each agent in order their ``worst'' set of goods according to the quota (instead of the demand).
elements.sent-2411-090: A mechanism that is truthful and neutral but not non-bossy, could let the first agent assign each agent her quota in order, but according to the demand determined by the preference of the first agent.
elements.sent-2411-091: Finally, a mechanism that is truthful and non-bossy but not neutral, could simply allocate the goods according to some constant allocation, irrespective of the agents reported preferences.
elements.sent-2411-092: Before discussing implication of our main result, we present the approach we take proving \cref{theorem:strict .}
elements.sent-2411-093: We prove it in several steps.
elements.sent-2411-094: We first prove the same characterization for {partition mechanisms over lexicographic preferences (which are always strict, meaning that there is never a tie between different subsets).}
elements.sent-2411-095: For these preferences, we first show that any truthful, non-bossy, and neutral partition mechanism must be Pareto-efficient (\cref{proposition:Pareto-efficient ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: This turns out to be sufficient to imply that the mechanism must be a serial-quota mechanism (\cref{proposition:lexi_case ).}
elements.sent-2411-097: We then move to consider any class $\PP$ in the family of strict classes $\FFn(\MM)$.
elements.sent-2411-098: We first prove a central lemma, called the Control Lemma (\cref{control ), which is a variant of the Control Lemma proven in \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 , which we later use to prove the characterization result for partition mechanisms over $\PP$.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The Control Lemma of \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 is based on two key concepts that they define.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The first is that of ``strong desire'' and that second is of ``control''.
elements.sent-2411-101: We extend the definition of ``strong desire'' to match the setting we study.
elements.sent-2411-102: In \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 , ``strong desire'' was defined only for agents with additive valuations over the goods.}
elements.sent-2411-103: According to their definition, an agent with an additive valuation function strongly desires a set of goods $S\subseteq \MM$ if her value for every individual good in $S$ is greater then the sum of the value of all the goods in $\MM\setminus S$.
elements.sent-2411-104: We adapt this definition to monotone ordinal preferences.
elements.sent-2411-105: An agent with a monotone preference strongly desires $S\subseteq \MM$, if for any set $A$ she receives, the agent strictly prefers adding any non-empty subset of $S$ to $A$, over adding the entirety of $\MM \setminus S$ to $A$.
elements.sent-2411-106: In essence, this means the agent will always benefit from exchanging all her goods outside of $S$ for even a single good from $S\setminus A$.
elements.sent-2411-107: For example, an agent with lexicographic preferences over the goods according to some ordering $e_1,\ldots,e_m$ will strongly desire every ``prefix" of this ordering, e.g., the sets $\{e_1\}, \{e_1,e_2\},\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}$ are strongly desired.
elements.sent-2411-108: The second key concept is of ``control''.
elements.sent-2411-109: Here, our definition remains the same as in \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 : an agent controls a set $S$ for mechanism $f$ if whenever she strongly desires $S$, she gets it whole (and possibly some additional goods).}
elements.sent-2411-110: The central result which connects these two concepts is the Control Lemma.
elements.sent-2411-112: If there exists a profile $\veco\in \PP^n$ such that all agents strongly desire $S$, and yet $S\subseteq f_i\veco$ for some $i\in \NN$, then agent $i$ controls $S$ with respect to $f$.
elements.sent-2411-113: Additionally, since any partition mechanism for $n=2$ agents is trivially non-bossy (neither agent can affect the others outcome without affecting their own), the requirement of non-bossiness was satisfied (implicitly), but not explicitly stated as a requirement.
elements.sent-2411-114: We require it and only consider mechanisms that are non-bossy.
elements.sent-2411-115: Finally, we prove the lemma for a wide range of ordinal preference classes, while the original version was proven for additive valuation functions.
elements.sent-2411-116: Thus, our version of the lemma is weaker for $n=2$ agents, but our result holds for all $n$ and for a wider range of preference classes.
elements.sent-2411-117: We also present counterexamples showing that all three properties of the mechanism (being truthful, non-bossy, and neutral) are necessary for $n\geq 3$ agents (\cref{lemma:control_tight ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: In \cite{AmanatidisBCM17 the Control Lemma is proven directly for truthful partition mechanisms for two agents.}
elements.sent-2411-119: Unfortunately, we could not find such a direct proof for $n\geq 3$ agents.
elements.sent-2411-120: Instead, our proof relies heavily on the full characterization of allocation mechanisms that are truthful, non-bossy, and neutral when the agents preferences are lexicographic (\cref{proposition:lexi_case ).}
elements.sent-2411-121: We are able to use this characterization to prove the Control Lemma for any preference class which contains the lexicographic preferences.
elements.sent-2411-122: We then show that the result can be extended to get the same characterization (as serial-quota mechanisms) not only for partition mechanisms but also for allocation mechanisms (that do not have to allocate all goods), using a reduction argument.
elements.sent-2411-123: We next discuss some implications of our characterization result for ordinal preferences, first to derive characterization results for cardinal preferences (valuations), and then also to derive strong impossibilities for truthful fair allocation of indivisible goods to agents with additive valuations (for mechanisms that are non-bossy and neutral).
elements.sent-2411-124: These impossibilities are in sharp contrast to known results which demonstrate that good fairness properties can be guaranteed by allocation algorithms (that are not truthful).
elements.sent-2411-125: Cardinal preferences, otherwise known as valuation functions (or simply valuations), are more nuanced than ordinal preferences.
elements.sent-2411-126: While ordinal preferences only provide information on the relative ranking of different subsets of goods, valuation functions provide a real number for each subset, capturing the (cardinal) utility of the subset.
elements.sent-2411-127: Despite this disparity in information, we prove that our characterization for strict ordinal preferences applies to strict cardinal preferences as well.
elements.sent-2411-128: Formally: \begin{restatable {application {cardinalstrict Let $f$ be an allocation mechanism of a set of goods $\MM$ among a set $\NN$ of $n$ agents with valuations from a class $\VV$ of strict (and monotone) valuation functions that is permutations-closed and induces every lexicographic preference, when not all goods need to be allocated.}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Mechanism $f$ is truthful, non-bossy, and neutral if and only if $f$ is a serial-quota mechanism on ${\VV}$.
elements.sent-2411-130: In particular, the additional information provided by the cardinal preference cannot be used to optimize the allocation.
elements.sent-2411-131: We also present an extension of this result to classes of valuations that might not be strict.
elements.sent-2411-132: For a valuation class $\VV$ of weakly-monotone valuation functions, we denote by $\VV_{\neq}$ the subset of strict valuations in $\VV$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Mechanism $f$ is truthful, non-bossy, and neutral on $\VV_{\neq}$ if and only if $f$ is a serial-quota mechanism on $\VV_{\neq}$.. \end{restatable.}
elements.sent-2411-134: For a class of valuations that contains valuations with ties, the above result shows that we know how every cardinal allocation mechanism that is truthful, non-bossy, and neutral must behave on the sub-class of strict valuations: it must behave like a serial-quota mechanism on these valuations.

elements.sent-2411-142: We next consider the fundamental envy-based fairness notion for indivisible items, that of envy-free-up-to-one-good (EF1).

elements.sent-2411-147: We discuss several directions for future work.

elements.sent-2411-157: The literature relevant to this article originates from two primary fields of study, {one on mechanism design and the other on fair allocation of indivisible goods .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Our paper is part of the vast literature on the design of allocation mechanisms in settings with private preferences (without transfers).
elements.sent-2411-159: We next discuss several papers in this literature that are most related to ours.
elements.sent-2411-160: A prominent model studied in the field of allocation of indivisible goods is the ``house allocation model"\cite{Shapely1974,Ma1994, Svensson1994, Svensson1999, Papai2000b , in which each agent can receive only one good.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Svensson \cite{Svensson1999 has presented a characterization result for this model, which is very similar to the one we prove for our model: it is shown that a mechanism for the house allocation model is truthful, non-bossy, and neutral if and only if it is serially-dictatorial (agents choose their house in turn according to some predefined ordering).}
elements.sent-2411-162: Our paper studies the more general and challenging problem, when agents have preferences over subsets and it is possible to allocate multiple goods to an agent, sometimes known as the ``multiple assignment" model.
elements.sent-2411-163: There have many different characterizations shown for the multiple assignment problem, each with different assumptions about the preferences of the agents and the properties the mechanism is assumed to posses.
elements.sent-2411-164: A common element in all these papers is the requirement that the mechanism be Pareto-efficient\footnote{In some papers (such as \cite{Svensson1999 ) Pareto-efficiency is not assumed outright but instead proven from the other assumptions.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: This is still fundamentally different from our characterization, which includes mechanisms that are not Pareto-efficient.. .
elements.sent-2411-166: Serial-quota mechanisms were first presented by Papai in \cite{Papai2000 , where it was shown that when agents preferences are strict and quantity-monotone (more is always better), an allocation mechanism strategy-proof, non-bossy, Pareto-efficient, and neutral if and only if it is a serial-quota mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-167: A similar result was shown when agents preferences are strict and monotone: an allocation mechanism strategy-proof, non-bossy, Pareto-efficient, and neutral if and only if it is a constrained serial-quota mechanism, where all agents but the first receive a quota of at most one good.
elements.sent-2411-168: The main contribution of our work over \cite{Papai2000 is removing the strong requirement of Pareto-efficiency, a significant constraint that implies that serial-quota mechanisms must have quotas at most 1 for all but the last agent.}

elements.subsection-2411-015: Necessity of all properties for the Control Lemma: {Proof of \cref{lemma:control_tight}}
elements.sent-2411-724: We present three allocations mechanisms, each violates only one of the three properties (of truthfulness, non-bossiness, and neutrality), s.t.
elements.sent-2411-725: Since the only preferences that $\PP$ is guaranteed to contain are the lexicographic preferences, we will only use lexicographic preferences in our counterexamples.
elements.sent-2411-726: Without truthfulness: Consider the following non-bossy and neutral partition mechanism of a set of goods $\MM=\{x,y\}$ among $n\geq 2$ agents.
elements.sent-2411-727: If both agents have the same preference, then agent $1$ gets all the goods.
elements.sent-2411-728: Otherwise, agent $2$ gets all the goods.
elements.sent-2411-729: The mechanism is non-bossy as any partition mechanism for 2 agents is non-bossy.
elements.sent-2411-730: The mechanism is neutral since the property ``the preferences are identical'' is invariant under permutation of the names of the goods.
elements.sent-2411-731: Yet the mechanism is not truthful because agent $2$ benefits from lying when both agents have identical preferences.
elements.sent-2411-732: Finally, notice that the control claim does not hold{, even for lexicographic preferences : over the profile where both agents have the same {{lexicographic preference which places the good $x$ highest, both agents strongly desire the singleton set $\{x\}$ and agent $1$ gets $x$, yet she does not control $x$ because if agent $2$ alters her reported preference she can take $x$, so this contradicts the control claim.}}}
elements.sent-2411-733: Without non-bossiness: Consider the following truthful and neutral partition mechanism of a set of goods $\MM$ among $n\geq 3$ agents.
elements.sent-2411-734: Let $x$ be the highest rated good according to agent $1$, then agent $2$ is allocated $\{x\}$ while agent $3$ is allocated all remaining goods.
elements.sent-2411-735: The mechanism is truthful because the only agent that affects the allocation is agent $1$, but she never gets any goods anyway so she can never have incentive to lie.
elements.sent-2411-736: The mechanism is neutral since permuting the names of the goods causes the best good $x$ to be permuted accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-737: Yet the mechanism is bossy, since when agent $1$ swaps her highest rated good she affects the allocation of agent $2$ without changing her own allocation.
elements.sent-2411-738: Finally, notice that the control claim does not hold{, even for lexicographic preferences : consider the case when all agents report {{lexicographic preferences {which place some good $x\in \MM$ the highest.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-739: Then all agents strongly desire the set $\{x\}$, and agent $2$ gets $\{x\}$ whole.
elements.sent-2411-740: Yet if agent $1$ reports some other preference which {places a different good the highest then agent $3$ gets $x$ instead, even though agent $2$ still strongly desires it, contradicting the control claim.}
elements.sent-2411-741: We remark that one can also modify this example to show that even with $n=2$ agents, a similar claim holds for allocations (not partitions).
elements.sent-2411-742: This can be done by instead of giving goods to agent $3$, simply throwing them away.
elements.sent-2411-743: Without neutrality: Consider the following truthful and non-bossy partition mechanism for allocating {a set $\MM$ of $m\geq 3$ goods among $n\geq 3$ agents.}
elements.sent-2411-745: Agent $1$ first picks her single favorite good $x$ from $\MM\setminus \{a\}$ and receives it.
elements.sent-2411-747: If $x=b$ then agent $2$ picks her single favorite good $y$ from $\MM\setminus \{x\}$ and agent $3$ gets all remaining goods $\MM\setminus\{x,y\}$.
elements.sent-2411-748: If $x\neq b$ then agent $3$ picks her single favorite good $y$ from $\MM\setminus \{x\}$ and agent $2$ gets all remaining goods $\MM\setminus\{x,y\}$.
elements.sent-2411-749: The mechanism is truthful since lying can only cause an agent to {get a less preferred good when choosing.}
elements.sent-2411-750: The mechanism is also non-bossy since the agents can only alter the overall allocation by altering the good they select at some step, thus altering their own allocation as well.
elements.sent-2411-751: Yet the mechanism is not neutral since it makes use of the specific naming of two goods $a,b$, so if we rename the goods according to a permutation that swaps the names of one of these goods with a different good the resulting allocation may be radically different.
elements.sent-2411-752: Finally, notice that control claim does not hold{, even for lexicographic preferences : consider two different profiles when all agents {have lexicographic preferences which place good $a$ the highest, and thus strongly desire the singleton set $\{a\}$, s.t.}}
